location: src/utils/sessionActions.js:481-483 (endLegacySession catch); callers at 221-223; sibling endSession catch at 411-415; logError import at line 5
reasoning: Confirmed from current source. Line 481-483: the endLegacySession catch contains ONLY `console.error("Error in endLegacySession:", err);` — no logError, no rethrow. logError is imported (line 5) and used 15+ times in this same file. The guarded write is updateDoc(userRef, updates) at line 469, which clears breakState.isTakingBreak / callState.isCalling / quickWorkState.isQuickWorking (lines 458-466) — the very flags that keep a worker stuck in a phantom session. The sibling endSession catch (lines 411-415) was deliberately upgraded to `logError(err, { source: 'endSession', userId })` with a comment stating exactly this rationale ("swallowed its failure... never logged it... no durable trace. Record it."), proving the asymmetry is the defect. Callers use `return await endLegacySession(...)` (lines 221-223); because endLegacySession's catch returns undefined (no rethrow), the awaited promise resolves normally, so the outer endSession catch never fires and no logError runs anywhere. errorLog.js confirms logError's two durable sinks (localStorage ring buffer MAX_ENTRIES=30 + Firestore error_logs) — neither is reached on this failure path. Net: on a failed flag-clear the worker stays stuck AND the breakage leaves no trace in either sink.
fixRisk: Very low. logError is import-present, side-effect-isolated (cannot throw — wrapped in try/catch and best-effort sinks), and already used on the identical sibling path, so behavior parity is established. It is a pure additive observability change: no control-flow change, no rethrow added (matching the sibling's deliberate no-rethrow choice so callers behave identically), no rules/index/deploy needed (error_logs sink already in use across the file). Client-only.

location: src/pages/ManagerView.jsx (missing hooks; mount after the useTaskTimeMonitor at ~line 74) vs src/pages/WorkerView.jsx:64,68 (both hooks present)
reasoning: Proven from source. Dashboard.jsx:44 routes mutually exclusively: `{isManagerRole(userRole) ? <ManagerView /> : <WorkerView />}`. WorkerView.jsx imports both hooks (lines 30-31) and mounts them: useOrphanedTaskRecovery(tasks) line 64, useOrphanedSessionRecovery(currentUser) line 68. ManagerView.jsx: grep for `useOrphaned|Recovery` returns NO matches — neither imported nor mounted. Managers CAN start secondary sessions: BottomNavigation.jsx renders <QuickWorkTimer/>, <CallTimer/>, <BreakTimer/> unconditionally in the floating work-controls pill (lines 74-76, inside an always-rendered div, no role gate); SideRail is the desktop equivalent shell (Layout.jsx:111/148 mount one or the other by viewport, never role). QuickWorkTimer's only role check (isManagerRole at line 211) merely empties the finish-prompt manager picker — it does NOT block starting the timer. useOrphanedSessionRecovery reads role-agnostic per-user state (userData.activeSession / quickWorkState / callState / breakState, lines 18-33), so recovery is fully meaningful for a manager. Net: a manager who starts a quickWork/call/break (or own-task) timer, then crashes/reloads, has the session left running with no auto-end and no RecoveryNotice — exactly the silent-ghost-time / capped multi-day-credit failure the hooks exist to prevent.
fixRisk: Low risk, client-only, no rules/deploy. Both hooks are idempotent (handledRef / once-per-app-session guards) and only act on sessions whose startTime predates APP_LOAD_TIME, so mounting them in ManagerView cannot disturb a live in-session timer. Passing ownTasks (manager's own task rows) is the correct analogue to WorkerView's tasks — it scopes task-recovery to the manager's own timers. No double-recovery risk since the two views never mount simultaneously (mutually exclusive route). Should be covered by the existing lint+build+test gate before ship.

location: src/components/Reports.jsx:426-440 (start/end build + the `d < start || d > end` filter); helpers in src/utils/timeUtils.js (vilniusWallClockToISO:397, getLithuanianDateString:218)
reasoning: getLithuanianDateString (timeUtils.js:218-226) yields a Vilnius `YYYY-MM-DD`, so taskFilters.startDate/endDate are date-only strings. `new Date("YYYY-MM-DD")` parses as UTC midnight, so `start` is anchored to UTC midnight — NOT Vilnius midnight. `end.setHours(23,59,59)` mutates in BROWSER-local time, so `end` is anchored to local end-of-day. completedAt/archivedAt/updatedAt are full UTC ISO (line 437), compared as absolute instants. The two boundaries use different reference frames. For a Vilnius user (UTC+3 summer) selecting endDate=2026-06-24: end = 2026-06-24T23:59:59 local = 2026-06-24T20:59:59Z, so tasks completed local 23:00–23:59 on the 24th (20:00:00Z–20:59:59Z is in, but 21:00:00Z–23:59:59Z = local 24th... actually local 23:00–23:59 = 20:00–20:59Z is included while the genuine end-of-Vilnius-day 21:00–23:59Z = next-day-local is excluded) — net effect: the last ~3 Vilnius hours of the selected day are dropped, and the lower bound is similarly off by the UTC/Vilnius gap. So tasks near the day boundary are wrongly in/excluded exactly as claimed, and no Vilnius helper is used here. The repo already provides vilniusWallClockToISO (timeUtils.js:397) and addDaysToDateString (:239) — DST-safe — so the fix is a drop-in.
fixRisk: Low risk, client-only (no rules/index/deploy). vilniusWallClockToISO and addDaysToDateString are pure, DST-safe (noon-reference offset), already covered by tests. Switching `> end` to `>= end` with an exclusive next-day-00:00 bound preserves inclusive end-day semantics without an off-by-one. Only behavioral change is correctly including/excluding the previously-mis-bounded ~couple of hours near each edge; result counts for boundary days may shift slightly (the intended correction). Guard: vilniusWallClockToISO returns null on malformed input — startDate/endDate are always produced by getLithuanianDateString so they match the YYYY-MM-DD regex, but a defensive fallback to the old Date() is cheap if desired. Run lint+build+vitest gate after.

fix: In getDateStr, swap the UTC slice for the Vilnius-day helper (already imported at Reports.jsx:4 and used at lines 54/81/82/326/936): replace `return dateStr.split('T')[0];` with `return getLithuanianDateString(dateStr);`. The `if (!dateStr) return 'No Date';` guard above stays; getLithuanianDateString accepts the ISO string directly.
reasoning: CONFIRMED the code defect, REFUTED the comparison premise. getDateStr (Reports.jsx:626-630) buckets a task by `(t.completedAt||t.archivedAt||t.updatedAt).split('T')[0]` = the UTC calendar date of the stored ISO. Vilnius is UTC+2/+3, so a task completed 00:00–02:59 Vilnius (21:00–23:59 prior UTC day in summer) is dated to the previous day in the grouped view; getLithuanianDateString (timeUtils.js:218-226, Intl with timeZone Europe/Vilnius) is the correct fix and is ALREADY imported and used in this same file. So the genuine UTC-vs-Vilnius mis-dating is real. HOWEVER the claim that this 'disagrees with DailyStatistics 03:00 boundary' is FALSE: DailyStatistics' 03:00/getLithuanian3AMCutoff window applies only to WORK SESSIONS (which carry a precomputed Vilnius `date`); its TASK bucketing (DailyStatistics.jsx:304-306) uses the IDENTICAL `t.completedAt?.split('T')[0]` raw-UTC pattern. The two surfaces are thus CONSISTENT on tasks, not in conflict. Severity is low, not medium: the boundary that actually shifts is UTC midnight (02:00/03:00 Vilnius), so only tasks finished in that narrow late-night window mis-group; the fix also makes Reports correct ahead of DailyStatistics. Note: a fully consistent fix would apply the same swap to DailyStatistics.jsx:304-306, but that is out of scope for this finding.
fixRisk: Very low. Pure client-side display grouping; no Firestore/rules/index change, no deploy. getLithuanianDateString returns the same YYYY-MM-DD shape, so the descending localeCompare sort (line 641) and any 'No Date' bucket are unaffected. Behavior changes only for tasks whose completion instant straddles UTC midnight (Vilnius ~02:00–03:00), moving them to the correct Vilnius day. One residual inconsistency remains until DailyStatistics.jsx:304-306 gets the same swap — but that pre-existed and is a separate finding.

location: src/utils/taskActions.js:27-29 (catch in updateUserWorkStatus); callers: pauseTask line 141, deleteTask line 513
reasoning: Source confirms all three sub-claims. (1) updateUserWorkStatus catch (lines 27-29) is console.error only — no logError, no rethrow. (2) logError is unambiguously the established pattern in this file: startTask (line 79), pauseTask (line 187), resumeTask (line 240), and the work_sessions write (line 167) all route failures through logError(err, {source:...}); updateUserWorkStatus is the lone async DB writer that does not. (3) The desync is provable: because the function catches and swallows its own rejection, a failed user-doc updateDoc returns a RESOLVED promise. So in pauseTask the task-doc updateDoc (line 129) commits while the user workStatus write silently fails, and pauseTask's own Promise.all (line 171) never sees a rejection, so its catch/logError (line 187) never fires. Same in deleteTask (line 513, awaited but swallowed). Result: user doc keeps stale workStatus (e.g. isWorking:true/running) against a paused/idle task, with zero trace in error_logs. The condition self-heals on the next start/resume (which overwrite the whole workStatus object), which is why this is medium, not high.
fixRisk: Very low. logError is best-effort and isolated (errorLog.js documents it can never throw), already imported, and used identically four times in the same file. Adding it does not change control flow — the catch still swallows and does not rethrow, preserving the intended non-fatal behavior of the user-status write relative to pause/delete. No rules/deploy involved; pure client-side observability addition. No human-only step.

location: src/utils/sessionActions.js:376-378 (catch in doResume guard); harmful overwrite at src/utils/taskActions.js:200-228 resumeTask
reasoning: Control flow confirmed in current source. doResume (sessionActions.js:346) iterates resumableTaskIds; for each it fetches the task doc, then runs a SAFEGUARD that getDoc's the latest users/{userId} doc and sets userStartedAnotherTask=true if a live non-task session exists (363-369), or a different active taskId is present (370-373). The resume condition at 380-385 gates on `!userStartedAnotherTask`. The guard getDoc is wrapped in try/catch (357-378); on throw the catch only console.warn's (376-377) and leaves the flag at its initialized false (356), so the resume proceeds. resumeTask (taskActions.js:200-228) then unconditionally overwrites users/{userId}.activeSession and workStatus to the OLD task. If the user had just started a new quick-work/call/break (non-task) session, pauseOtherTasks (203) doesn't touch it and the overwrite silently wipes the live secondary session with no pause record — exactly the harm the in-code comment at 363-368 warns about. So the guard's own documented failure mode is bypassed precisely when its fetch fails. Genuine defect: a safeguard that fails open defeats its purpose.
fixRisk: Low risk, client-only, no rules/deploy. Trade-off: failing closed means a transient guard-fetch hiccup can skip resuming a legitimately-paused old task — but this whole path is best-effort fire-and-forget (line 397, no await; line 388 already logs benign skips), and a non-resumed paused task is recoverable (user/UI can resume it, and orphan-recovery exists), whereas a wiped live session is silent data loss. Severity is low because the harmful branch requires a race (new session started) AND a transient throw on the second getDoc, after the first getDoc in the same closure succeeded — an uncommon conjunction. Add/adjust a unit test in sessionActions.test.js asserting a rejecting users getDoc skips resumeTask.

fix: Replace both raw <button>s with canonical Button. Add `import Button from './ui/Button';`. Mute → `<Button variant="secondary" size="md" icon={BellOff} onClick={handleMute} disabled={muted}>{muted ? 'Nutildyta' : 'Nutildyti garsą'}</Button>` (Button's icon renders w-5 h-5 vs the original w-4 h-4 — acceptable, or keep custom). Acknowledge → `<Button ref={ackButtonRef} variant="danger" className="px-6" onClick={handleAcknowledge}>Supratau</Button>` (Button is forwardRef, so initialFocusRef={ackButtonRef} still works; className="px-6" preserves the wider padding over md's px-4).
reasoning: Both footer controls are raw <button> elements (lines 94-101 mute, 102-108 acknowledge) hand-rolling rounded-control, min-h-touch, font-semibold text-body, shadow-sm, hover, disabled:opacity-50, and focus-visible:ring — every one of which ui/Button.jsx owns. The mute button's classes (border border-line bg-surface-card hover:bg-surface-sunken) are an exact match for Button's `secondary` VARIANT; the acknowledge button's (bg-red-600 hover:bg-red-700) maps to `danger` (bg-feedback-danger=#DC2626/red-600, hover=feedback-danger-hover=#B91C1C/red-700). Swap is feasible: Button is forwardRef so the ackButtonRef focus-trap target survives, and supports disabled/icon/className override for the px-6 padding. Two minor cosmetic divergences (Button adds active:scale press + transition vs transition-colors; danger keeps ring-brand vs the raw ring-red-700) are deltas, not blockers. This is a real DESIGN_SYSTEM §8 'canonical components only' violation, but not an a11y/functional defect — the raw styling already meets 44px targets and visible focus rings, so it's consistency/maintainability drift only.
fixRisk: Low risk, client-only, no rules/deploy. Visual deltas after swap: (1) buttons gain Button's active:scale-[0.98] press feedback (improvement); (2) acknowledge focus ring changes red-700→brand (cosmetic); (3) if Button's `icon` prop is used for the bell, the icon grows w-4→w-5 — to preserve exact sizing, render the BellOff inline as a child instead of via `icon`. Must re-run `npm run lint` + `npm run build`. No behavior change to handleMute/handleAcknowledge/SoundManager. Safe for an agent to apply.

location: src/components/BreakTimer.jsx:102-122 (compact) & 152-177 (full); src/components/CallTimer.jsx:300-320 (compact) & 344-378 (desktop); src/components/QuickWorkTimer.jsx:363-383 (compact) & 407-440 (desktop); canonical Button at src/components/ui/Button.jsx:16-27 (VARIANTS/SIZES)
fix: Add a session-toggle affordance to the canonical Button (src/components/ui/Button.jsx) and migrate the six bespoke timer buttons to it. Concretely: introduce a `pressed` (boolean) + `session` ('quickWork'|'call'|'break'|'task') prop pair; when `pressed`, Button applies the active scaffold `bg-session-{session}-shell text-white ring-2 ring-session-{session}-soft` (mirroring QuickWorkTimer's compact active state) and otherwise the neutral resting `bg-surface-sunken text-ink hover:bg-line`. This collapses the repeated scaffold — `inline-flex items-center justify-center min-h-touch ... transition active:scale-[0.98] focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-brand focus-visible:ring-offset-2` — into one place. Then replace each timer's hand-rolled `<button className={clsx(...)}>` with `<Button pressed={isActive} session="break" icon={...} onClick={...} aria-label={...}>`. Note the desktop variants (CallTimer:344, QuickWorkTimer:407) are richer split-row layouts (icon column + label + mono timer); those need a distinct composite, so scope the first pass to the four compact buttons + BreakTimer's full button, which share one shape. Do NOT do a blind className swap — Button as written cannot express the session ring, so the component must be extended first.
reasoning: Claim confirmed against current source. (1) Duplication: all three timers hand-roll their session-toggle <button> in BOTH compact and full/desktop variants — six bespoke buttons. The four compact ones (BreakTimer:102, CallTimer:300, QuickWorkTimer:363) are near-identical: same `inline-flex items-center justify-center min-h-touch min-w-touch rounded-control transition-all active:scale-95` scaffold + the byte-identical focus-ring string `focus-visible:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-brand focus-visible:ring-offset-2`, differing only in the session token name and icon. (2) Button gap is real: src/components/ui/Button.jsx exposes only VARIANTS {primary,secondary,success,danger,ghost} and SIZES {md,lg} with a fixed brand focus ring and no toggle/pressed or session concept — so it cannot express the active state these buttons need (`ring-2 ring-session-{type}-soft` + session-shell fill). (3) The session-colored active ring is genuinely required: it is the product's signature 'loud state' cue, and the tokens it uses (session.{quickWork,call,break,task}.{shell,surface,accent,soft}) all exist in tailwind.config.js:51-54. So this is a true maintainability/discipline finding (duplicated raw scaffold, canonical component exists) — but NOT a defect: no token violation (all classes are valid tokens, no raw hex/arbitrary px), touch targets are min-h-touch (44px), focus rings present, color is never the sole signal (icon shape + aria-label change per state). Hence low severity: a refactor opportunity, not a user-facing bug.
fixRisk: Human-judgment refactor, not a mechanical swap, so it carries regression risk if rushed: the desktop variants (CallTimer:344, QuickWorkTimer:407) are split-row composites (icon block + uppercase label + live mono timer) that do NOT fit Button's icon+children shape, and CallTimer's active desktop state additionally pulls its accent from getSessionColors('call') and uses ring-1 ring-line rather than the soft-token ring — so a one-size Button cannot absorb all six without either bloating Button's API or leaving the desktop pair behind. Lowest-risk path: extend Button with `pressed`/`session`, migrate only the four compact buttons + BreakTimer's full button (one shared shape), leave the two desktop composites as-is. Pure client-side UI/Tailwind change — no Firestore/rules/functions, no deploy, no human-only boundary. After change, re-verify the active-state ring/fill renders per session at a ~360px viewport and run the lint+build+test gate.

fix: Acknowledge button (line 105): swap `bg-red-600` -> `bg-feedback-danger`, `hover:bg-red-700` -> `hover:bg-feedback-danger-hover`, `focus-visible:ring-red-700` -> `focus-visible:ring-feedback-danger-hover`. Header (line 51): swap `from-red-600 to-red-700` -> `from-feedback-danger to-feedback-danger-hover`.
reasoning: Line 105 button className contains literal `bg-red-600 ... hover:bg-red-700 ... focus-visible:ring-red-700`; line 51 header contains `bg-gradient-to-r from-red-600 to-red-700`. Both are raw palette utilities, violating Rule B (every state color from the token map). Token equivalents exist and resolve to identical hex: tailwind.config.js:89-95 maps feedback.danger.{DEFAULT,hover} to CSS vars --fb-danger (index.css:88 = `220 38 38` = #DC2626 = red-600) and --fb-danger-hover (index.css:92 = `185 28 28` = #B91C1C = red-700). The same file already uses `bg-feedback-danger`/`text-feedback-danger` at line 80, proving the utilities compile. Not a contrast defect — white text on red-600/red-700 is fine and the header was deliberately darkened from red-500 (comment line 50). Pure token-discipline issue, hence low severity.
fixRisk: Visually a no-op: --fb-danger and --fb-danger-hover hold the same hex (#DC2626 / #B91C1C) in BOTH light and dark themes (index.css:88-92 and 146-150), so the gradient and button render pixel-identical after the swap. Pure className string replacement, no logic touched, no rules/deploy. Safe to apply directly.

fix: In QuickWorkDescribePrompt.jsx:40, swap the raw utility for the session token: change `border border-red-200` -> `border border-session-quickWork-soft`. Full className becomes: `mb-5 bg-session-quickWork-surface rounded-card p-4 border border-session-quickWork-soft flex items-center justify-between`.
reasoning: Line 40 renders the elapsed-time card with `bg-session-quickWork-surface ... border border-red-200`. tailwind.config.js:51 defines session.quickWork.soft = #FECACA, identical to Tailwind's stock red-200 (#FECACA), so the swap is pixel-for-pixel identical (zero visual change). The sibling, visually mirrored card in QuickWorkTimer.jsx:61 already uses `border-session-quickWork-soft` for the same surface — confirming the canonical token exists and is the intended choice. tailwind.config.js:49 even comments the anti-pattern explicitly: "reaching for raw border-red-200 ... outside the token system." This is a genuine token-discipline violation per DESIGN_SYSTEM (tokens, not magic colors).
fixRisk: Zero runtime/visual risk: source value is byte-identical (#FECACA both sides) so rendering is unchanged; it only restores token discipline and parity with the sibling component. Client-only className edit, no rules/deploy, no human-only boundary. Re-run lint/build per the quality gate.

location: firestore.rules:498-503 (match /error_logs/{logId}, create at :499); client write src/utils/errorLog.js:62-68, 91-101
fix:
Replace the bare create rule with the permissive size-clamp idiom already used by request_notifications (:454) and decision_log (:524). Old:
  allow create: if isAuthenticated();
New:
  allow create: if isAuthenticated()
                && (!('message' in request.resource.data) || (request.resource.data.message is string && request.resource.data.message.size() <= 4000))
                && (!('stack' in request.resource.data) || (request.resource.data.stack is string && request.resource.data.stack.size() <= 16000))
                && (!('componentStack' in request.resource.data) || (request.resource.data.componentStack is string && request.resource.data.componentStack.size() <= 16000))
                && (!('url' in request.resource.data) || (request.resource.data.url is string && request.resource.data.url.size() <= 4000))
                && (!('userAgent' in request.resource.data) || (request.resource.data.userAgent is string && request.resource.data.userAgent.size() <= 1000))
                && (!('source' in request.resource.data) || (request.resource.data.source is string && request.resource.data.source.size() <= 200))
                && (request.resource.data.get('userId', null) == null || request.resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid);
(Bounds chosen above the client clamps in errorLog.js so the legit append passes unchanged: message<=2000, stack/componentStack<=8000; url/userAgent are currently UNbounded client-side so the rule is the only cap. The userId guard tolerates null because errorLog.js sets userId=null when auth is not ready.) HUMAN-ONLY: this is a Firestore rules change — deploy is post-ship from an up-to-date main checkout, then re-verify the live ruleset via the Firebase MCP.
reasoning: firestore.rules:499 is literally `allow create: if isAuthenticated();` with no field/size/provenance constraints — claim confirmed verbatim. read at :500 is isManagerOrAdmin(), so any injected/oversized content reaches a manager-visible surface. errorLog.js:62-68 writes via addDoc as fire-and-forget; the record (:91-101) clamps message(2000)/stack(8000)/componentStack(8000) CLIENT-SIDE only, while url (window.location.href, :97) and userAgent (navigator.userAgent, :98) are written UNBOUNDED. Client clamps are advisory: any authenticated caller using the SDK directly bypasses errorLog.js entirely and can write arbitrary-size/malformed docs (long-string injection, doc-size bloat, floodable since there is no provenance pin tying userId to auth.uid). The exact permissive idiom to fix this already exists in two sibling rules: request_notifications:456-464 and decision_log:525-534 both use type-check + .size() bound + `(!('field' in data) || ...)` and tolerate null/absent fields. Applying it here does NOT break the legit append — all fields errorLog.js writes are strings within the proposed bounds, and the userId guard mirrors decision_log's null-tolerant `reason` pattern (errorLog.js sets userId=null when auth.currentUser is unavailable, so a hard `userId==auth.uid` pin would wrongly reject offline/early-crash logs — hence the `== null ||` branch).
fixRisk: Low risk to legit traffic: bounds set above the client clamps so normal appends pass; the only behavioral tightening is rejecting docs that exceed the caps or spoof a non-self userId. One real footgun to avoid: do NOT hard-pin userId==auth.uid — errorLog.js writes userId=null when auth is not ready (the very early-crash case the durable log exists for), so the guard must keep the `userId==null ||` branch or it will silently drop the most important crash reports. HUMAN-ONLY: rules deploy is irreversible against the one shared prod project and must happen post-ship from an up-to-date main checkout, with the live ruleset re-verified via firebase_get_security_rules (not the deploy log). Validate first with firebase_validate_security_rules before any deploy.

fix: Remove `opacity-70` from both sub-label spans on lines 434-435. Old: ` · nuo {startLabel}` and ` · planas {plannedTime}` -> New: ` · nuo {startLabel}` and ` · planas {plannedTime}`. With opacity removed the inherited session-accent color alone passes AA (4.57:1 task .. 5.91:1 quickWork). If a softer look is wanted, use a token color that itself passes (e.g. text-ink-muted at full opacity = 4.83:1) rather than opacity on the accent.
reasoning: The claim's stated premise (div inherits --ink-muted) is WRONG, but the conclusion holds and is worse. Walking the tree: the card root (line 408) applies `session.colorClass`, which for every real session type = `${...surface} ${...accent}` (lines 111/119/127/142), i.e. bg-session-*-surface + text-session-*-accent. The sub-label div (432) and its spans inherit that ACCENT text color, not ink-muted. Accent-on-surface (12px text, needs >=4.5:1) starts at only: quickWork #B91C1C/#FEF2F2=5.91, call #2563EB/#EFF6FF=4.75, break #B45309/#FFFBEB=4.84, task #15803D/#DCFCE7=4.57. The `opacity-70` span alpha-blends the accent 0.7 over the surface, dropping the effective ratio to 3.62/2.87/2.89/2.74 respectively — ALL below 4.5:1 = AA FAIL. The default/stale fallback (text-ink #374151 on surface-sunken #F3F4F6) drops from 9.37 to 4.12, also a fail. Computed via WCAG relative-luminance with proper sRGB alpha compositing. Sub-labels carry real info (start time, plan budget), so this is meaningful body text, not decoration.
fixRisk: Very low. Pure className edit, no logic change, no rules/deploy. Removing opacity-70 only makes the existing accent text fully opaque; the accent-on-surface base already passes AA for every session type and the dimming was the sole cause of failure. Visual effect: the secondary sub-labels become slightly bolder/darker, which is the intended a11y improvement. No other component reads these spans.

location: src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:996 and :1157 (the two failing lines). Non-failing cited lines: 889, 918, 963 (info-text, 4.6:1 PASS), 1221 (ink, 5.71:1 PASS).
fix: Remove `opacity-80` from the two warning-card paragraphs. Line 996: `mt-2 text-xs italic opacity-80 border-l-2 border-feedback-warning-border pl-2` -> `mt-2 text-xs italic border-l-2 border-feedback-warning-border pl-2`. Line 1157: `mt-1 text-xs italic opacity-80 border-l-2 border-feedback-warning-border pl-2` -> `mt-1 text-xs italic border-l-2 border-feedback-warning-border pl-2`. The italic + left border already provide the quoted-text de-emphasis, so the opacity is redundant. (The info-text and ink lines may keep opacity-80 — they stay >=4.5:1.)
reasoning: The claim's hypothesis (ink-muted base) is wrong, but the failure is real with a corrected cause. The cards inherit color from a parent div, not ink-muted. Lines 996/1157 sit inside `text-feedback-warning-text` (amber-700 #B45309) on `bg-feedback-warning-soft` (amber-50 #FFFBEB). Amber-700 on amber-50 is only 4.84:1 at full opacity; the `opacity-80` composites the text over its own amber-50 bg, dropping it to 3.43:1 — below AA 4.5:1 for 12px (text-xs) text. The info-text lines (889/918/963) inherit indigo-700 #4338CA on indigo-50 #EEF2FF (7.07:1 full), which at 0.8 = 4.6:1 and PASS. The fallback line 1221 inherits ink #374151 on white = 5.71:1 at 0.8, PASS. Dark theme passes on every line (warning 6.39:1, info 5.64:1) because the soft bg is near-black. So exactly 2 of the 6 cited lines fail, both warning cards, light theme only.
fixRisk: Safe, client-only className edit; no token/rules/deploy change. Removing opacity-80 slightly increases ink weight of the quoted reason/description text, but italic + the colored left rule preserve the visual hierarchy that distinguishes it from the headline. No layout shift. Applies in both themes harmlessly (dark already passed; opacity removal only raises contrast there too).

fix: No fix required. If consistency is desired later: the only optional change is replacing the raw CSS breakpoint pair (md:hidden list / hidden md:block table) with the JS viewMode switch used by the top-level lists — purely cosmetic, not a defect. No className/color swap is needed for AA.
reasoning: A mobile card fallback DOES exist: lines 1409-1461 are an explicit `<ul className="... md:hidden">` ("Mobile: timeline as a card list ... never a horizontal table on a phone (§9)") using text-caption(12px)/text-body(14px) with text-ink/text-ink-muted. The cited text-xs table at :1464 is `hidden overflow-x-auto md:block` — the DESKTOP-ONLY counterpart, correctly paired with md:hidden on the list. This IS the design system's dual-density §9 pattern (cards on phone, dense table on md+), not a static/broken breakpoint. The table rows (:1478) inherit text-ink-muted #6B7280 (break rows: feedback-warning-text) on white surface-card; text-xs=12px is the allowed floor. Computed contrast: #6B7280 on #FFFFFF = 4.83:1 (per index.css:194), and 12px<18.66px needs >=4.5:1 -> 4.83:1 PASSES. No contrast fail, no size fail, mobile fallback present. The claim itself concedes 12px is the floor and not a contrast fail.
fixRisk: None. Nothing to change. An optional cosmetic refactor to the JS viewMode pattern would touch render branching for no a11y benefit and risks regressing the working §9 card/table split, so it is not worth doing.

fix: On both label spans, swap the confirmed color token: `isConfirmed ? "text-feedback-success" : "text-ink-muted"` -> `isConfirmed ? "text-feedback-success-text" : "text-ink-muted"`. This is exactly the AA-safe green-700 token already used on the completedAt chip two lines above (line 2034). The muted branch and the green Check icon/checkbox (lines 2035, 2050) need no change.
reasoning: The finding's premise (muted/opacity 12px text failing contrast) is wrong for most cited lines but a genuine, narrower failure exists. Computed real ratios at 12px (<18.66px, AA needs >=4.5:1): (1) description 2005 `text-ink-muted` #6B7280 on white = 4.83:1 PASS; (2) comment preview 2090 `text-ink-muted` on explicit bg-surface-card #FFFFFF = 4.83:1 PASS; (3) comment-count 2083 inherits IconButton ink, bold 12px PASS. BUT the confirmed-status labels at 2052/2057 use `text-feedback-success` = `--fb-success` = green-600 #16A34A (NOT the `.text`/green-700 variant). The green branch only renders when `isConfirmed`, and isConfirmed sets the card bg to `bg-surface-sunken` #F3F4F6 (line 1987). Green-600 (L=0.2685) on #F3F4F6 (L=0.898) = 2.98:1 — FAILS AA for 12px text. The AA-safe replacement `--fb-success-text` = green-700 #15803D yields 4.56:1 on the same sunken bg (PASS) and is ALREADY used for the completedAt chip text at line 2034, so it's the established in-file pattern. Inconsistency confirmed by grep: 2034 uses `text-feedback-success-text`, 2052/2057 use raw `text-feedback-success`. Dark theme is unaffected (green-600 on dark sunken #222936 = 4.68:1). 12px is the floor, not a violation; multi-line 12px prose is sanctioned (text-caption=12px). So the only real defect is the green-600 confirmed-label color in light theme.
fixRisk: Pure className token swap, light-theme contrast only; no logic change. Safe — `text-feedback-success-text` already resolves to a defined token (green-700 light / green-400 dark) and is used elsewhere in the same file, so dark theme stays AA too. Client-only, no rules/deploy. The only visual effect: the "Priimtas" label darkens slightly from green-600 to green-700, which is the intended AA correction. Leave the checkbox accent and Check icon on `text-feedback-success` (decorative/non-text, larger).

fix: In the timestamp span at line 153, swap the color token (and tidy the size token): `<span className="text-xs text-ink-muted">` -> `<span className="text-caption text-ink">`. The `text-ink` swap is what fixes the contrast; `text-xs`->`text-caption` is the canonical 12px/16px token (same 12px size). If keeping the muted look is preferred, an alternative that also clears AA is to keep `text-ink-muted` but it does NOT pass on the sunken card, so darkening is required.
reasoning: The comment card wrapper at line 147 is `bg-surface-sunken` (#F3F4F6), and the timestamp at lines 153-155 is `text-xs text-ink-muted` (#6B7280) at full opacity, 12px (text-xs, < 18.66px so needs >=4.5:1). The prompt's premise (4.83:1) is the ratio on WHITE; the real inherited background here is the sunken card, not white. Computed contrast of #6B7280 on #F3F4F6 = 4.393:1, below the 4.5:1 AA floor (#6B7280 on white = 4.834:1, which is why it passes elsewhere but not here). So this is a genuine, marginal AA fail driven by the non-white card background, plus a minor token-discipline point (text-xs vs the canonical text-caption, both 12px). Not the false positive the brief anticipated.
fixRisk: Pure className change, client-only, no rules/deploy. `text-ink` (#374151) on sunken is ~9:1, comfortably AA. Visual side-effect: the timestamp becomes a touch darker/heavier in the comment header row — acceptable for meta text and improves legibility outdoors. `text-caption` is the same 12px so no layout shift. The identical pattern with ink-muted-on-sunken appears elsewhere in this file (e.g. TimeAdjustments reason line 367, checklist doneBy line 283) but those are larger or non-critical meta; not changed by this minimal fix.

fix: In the button className at line 327, swap `before:-inset-y-[9px]` -> `before:-inset-y-[10px]`. This makes the tap target 24px (h-6) + 10 + 10 = 44px, hitting the AA/DESIGN_SYSTEM floor exactly. Optionally update the comment numbers (9px->10px, "2px clearance"->"0px clearance / abutting").
reasoning: Confirmed from source. Visible pill = h-6 = 24px (lines 305/307). Interactive drill-down button (line 327) extends the hit area via `before:-inset-y-[9px]` = +9px top and bottom. 24 + 9 + 9 = 42px, 2px below the binding 44px touch target. Row pitch: each row is h-8 (32px) + space-y-3 gap (12px) = 44px; bar sits at top-1 (4px), height 24px. With -inset-y-[9px], adjacent bars' hit targets leave exactly 2px clearance (matches the code comment). With -inset-y-[10px] (=44px target), the clearance shrinks to exactly 0px — targets abut but do NOT overlap, so the +10px fix is geometrically safe. Real defect: provably under 44px for an interactive control.
fixRisk: Very low risk, client-only CSS, no logic/data change, no rules/deploy. Only side effect: vertically-adjacent drill-down hit targets go from 2px apart to exactly touching (0px). They abut but never overlap, so there is no mis-tap of the wrong row; the visible 24px pill is unchanged so no visual regression. Only the interactive (manager canDrillUser) bar gets the extended target; the non-interactive worker div (line 335) has no ::before and is unaffected.
